summary: I was born in 1955 in Mönchengladbach, Germany, a city in the northwest near Holland. I grew up there with my mother, older brother, and sister. Mönchengladbach is where my early life and roots are, before I eventually moved abroad.

I met my wife while hitchhiking on a trip to New Zealand, which was a memorable moment in my life. That trip led to a lifelong partnership and eventually to starting a family. New Zealand was a key place in my personal journey before settling down in Berlin and later Minnesota.

My wife and I lived in Berlin for several years, where we had our first two daughters. I loved working there as an architect, which was my profession before moving to the United States. Berlin was an important chapter in both my family life and career.

In 2000, I moved my family to Minnesota to be closer to my wife's family. Minnesota became home, even though I was disappointed by the lack of good German-style bread. After losing my architecture job in 2009 due to the recession, Minnesota is where I started baking bread and eventually opened my bakery.

Minneapolis is where I opened Aki's BreadHaus in 2014 after years of baking as a hobby. It’s a city where I’ve been able to share traditional German bread and pretzels with the community. I’ve seen a great response from locals and German Americans who appreciate a taste of home here.

Before opening my own bakery, I worked out of a shared space in Osseo, Minnesota. It was an important step in growing my bread business from a hobby to something more serious. Osseo gave me the space and opportunity to develop my recipes and start selling at farmers markets.

Eventually, I had the chance to work with a co-op in Northeast Minneapolis, which helped me open Aki's BreadHaus. This neighborhood has been a great location for my bakery and has a strong community of German Americans and European Americans. We’ve almost outgrown this space because of the positive feedback and loyal customers.

I founded Aki's BreadHaus in Minneapolis in May 2014 after losing my architecture job. It started from baking bread for my family and grew into a bakery offering over fifteen kinds of bread and German soft pretzels. The bakery has become a place where people come for authentic German bread, pastries, and pretzels, and it’s been rewarding to bring a slice of Germany to the community.

Oktoberfest is a big part of our business, especially because we sell almost a thousand German soft pretzels every week to local breweries around the metro area to go with beer. It reminds me of the traditional German celebrations where pretzels and beer are staples. This event helps connect our bakery to German culture here in Minnesota.
